title: Should I eat my meat/organs raw or cooked?

chunks.title: Cooking and Nutrient Bioavailability
chunks.summary: This segment discusses the impact of cooking on the bioavailability of proteins and nutrients in various foods, particularly focusing on meat and organs like liver. The speaker explores the benefits of consuming certain foods raw to retain nutrients while also acknowledging the trade-offs associated with cooking for improved protein digestibility.

chunks.title: Raw Eggs and Avidin
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to eggs, emphasizing the importance of cooking egg whites to neutralize avidin, which can bind biotin. The speaker highlights the safety of consuming raw egg yolks if proper handling is observed and describes personal experiences with egg consumption.
chunks.transcript: would cook it if you're gonna eat the egg whites some people just for a while uh when i was eating more eggs i would just separate and just eat the yolks because i found them interesting to eat raw and enjoyable and simple the one thing i'll say about eggs is from the data that i've seen if you're getting an organic egg or a pasture raised egg if you know the quality the incidence of the salmonella and the campflow bacter on the shell is much lower and that's where the contamination comes from so if you crack the egg and there's contamination on the shell if you keep the yolk membrane intact the yolk has like a really thin membrane and you know how you can like crack an egg separate the white it'll fall through your hand the yolk will just sit in your hand it looks really cool it's like this little bubble and it's just it's not surface tension there's actually a membrane on the yolk and if you haven't cracked the yolk with a raw egg the risk of contamination there is extremely low because the shell didn't even really touch the yolk so that's one thing to consider but i would cook your whites if

chunks.title: Nutrient Deficiencies and Nose-to-Tail Eating
chunks.summary: This part discusses the potential benefits of nose-to-tail eating and its historical significance in hunter-gatherer diets, focusing on nutrients like biotin and riboflavin found in organ meats. The speaker suggests that incorporating these foods could enhance overall nutrient status.
chunks.transcript: some of your questions yeah yeah like i've seen when you actually look at it from like a molecular mass level it seems like the amount of biotin from the yolk itself relative to the evident content of the egg white surrounding it you could otherwise just be like netting out like zero where it's like you're not i don't know where it's like you or you could be at a net positive but at the same time like it seems like the more reasonable approach is just to ensure you've neutralized the avidin component by cooking it adequately yeah at least for the white um and then you'd have to go through and look how heat labile is biotin and the yolk how much of the you know how much of the biotin in the yolk are you going to denature when you cook it so and i think a lot of people could also benefit from more biotin which is one of the kind of overarching ideas and that's the whole reason that i got interested in in nose to tail eating and these hunter-gatherer cultures and eating organs or eggs egg yolks or anything in the first place like are there nutrients like biotin or riboflavin or ancient or taurine or coq10 that we could really improve our overall nutrient status by sort of eating in a way that is apparently evolutionarily consistent what do you think about the

chunks.title: Peptides and Growth Factors in Raw Foods
chunks.summary: The segment delves into the notion of retaining peptides and growth factors by consuming raw animal products, questioning their bioavailability and effectiveness. The discussion also touches on potential research gaps in understanding these compounds and their implications for human health.
